#24 Komine Park and the terrifying tunnel, Tokyo

March 20, 2024 1 hour 51 minutes

All transcripts are machine generated.

I got a couple hours of sunlight, which is not enough. But then I saw in the map, Kumine Park. So I'm like, oh, all right, that's pretty close and I'm walking around anyway. So let's go there and so far this place is very fucking nice. It's like this nice mix of, you know, it's very natural like this, like all these little paths have been walking down are all very nature-y. But at the same time, there's like a visitor center at the bottom and midway through a little guesthouse place. So it's got, you know, still got drink machines and it's still got visitors centers and washrooms. And it's just fucking gorgeous because we're into the mountains now. And every time, you know, you can peek out through the vegetation, you can just see for millions of miles. There's little tour buses that bring little groups. It seemed to be older people. They're mostly hanging out down at the bottom, having some kind of a little picnic. They're not heading up into the mountains. Okay, it's leveled out. It's swerved around. Oh, there we go. Back up. Back up. So it's so far. I mean, this is already totally worked. I got to take the cool train ride. I got to go to new days 35:00 and buy a bunch of fun crap to eat. And I wandered accidentally into a beautiful park. But yeah, this is extra good because central Tokyo is, I think why it's the capital, if I understand right, is because it's flat. That's why it has developed so well because Japan is extremely mountainous. And apparently for long distance driving and stuff, highway tolls and things are supposed to be terrible because it's so expensive to go around or through mountains and stuff.

So I think Tokyo is good because it's flat. But not flat is much more interesting to walk around. So this is cool. I'm into a bunch of really cool parks. But this one, man, yeah, I'm like, it is severe. How steep this is and just like heading up. I mean, it's not like a Dutch staircase, but this is unusual. Usually these slopes are a lot more gentle.

Oh, here we go though. It's balancing out again.

Get a very distinct Indiana Jones feeling. I'm just going through the jungle.

So yeah, that's something I wanted to bring up. So yesterday, I was on Spider Mountain. And I was saying how those spiders do remind me of old Sega Master System games. You know, a lot of the animals in video games I played as a kid because those games came from Japan. Now I'm recognizing. I was like, oh yeah, those are the animals.

I gotta take another break here.

But I was getting pretty freaked out by the sheer number of spiders on that mountain. It was like frightening. And one of the things that was in my mind a little just knowing nothing and being so ignorant of anything about spiders and these spiders in particular is I was thinking like, okay, so if all the games I played as a kid had spider enemies and they look a lot like these spiders, another thing that many spiders do in video games is they drop. They fall from the trees onto you. And that was definitely in my mind as well. And I decided to bail out and stop trying to make my way through that mountain. But I looked it up when I got home. So if anyone's curious, those spiders, it's the Joro Spider, J-O-R-O Spider. And my mind was calmed considerably just by doing a little research. It reminds me of after hiking through a bunch of mountains in Fukuoka. It wasn't until afterward after I ran into a couple of boars and things that it occurred to me. Maybe I should be concerned about bears because there's a YouTube channel Go North Japan where he talks often about how to protect yourself against bears while hiking. And I was like, man, am I just wandering into the woods completely unprepared like a stupid fuck? But in that case, I was glad to learn. Bears are only in northern Japan. In Kiyushu, I believe is the name of that southern island that Fukuoka is on. There are no bears in Kiyushu. It's too warm. So similarly here, there are 50,000 different types of spiders in Japan. And there's only one dangerous one, the classic black widow, which is pretty easy to identify because it's black, kind of circular, with red markings on its back. And they're still pretty rare, but they are around. So that's something to be aware of. But the other 50,000 types are fine. Oh, fuck, it's still going up. Another rest, I'm just going to just stop at each of these plateaus. Oh, I guess this could go up pretty far because I'm looking ahead of me through the trees. And there's like power lines, the big super industrial power line towers. And there's a mountain much higher than that. So if this mountain is comparable, no wonder the old folk are staying at the bottom. I did pass some people like a family, I think, that were coming down the mountain with their dog. But yeah, these other spiders are not dangerous. So it's just like I was saying back home in Canada, I always like spiders. I'm glad to have them around because anything a spider is eating is something that I would prefer. I'd prefer to have the spider than the thing that it's getting rid of. Spiders are good. So these ones too, and they're beautiful. They're so pretty. They're black and yellow mostly with some green and some red. The Joro spider, give it a look, it looks awesome. And now I know I don't have to be scared of them. I think I still would have bailed out of that mountain because they were just too many of them everywhere. The webs were just in the way. It was getting unpleasant to navigate.

But at least that little back in my brain thing of like, am I just really doing something dangerous? You know, at least now I know I was not. Apparently too. It's amazing how things can spread in the world. I believe I read that the black widows they think came to Japan from Australia on ships. And similarly, these Joro spiders in the last 10 years or so have started appearing in America. And again, probably on ships, but they don't know. They don't quite know where they are and how far they've spread or what effect they're having on the ecosystem. It seems okay so far because they've only been around 10 years and it's just hard to gauge these things. You know, we take for granted all the stuff that scientists have figured out already. But the process of finding out is very slow and gradual and lacking in actual information.

But speaking of spiders falling from the sky, apparently these Joro spiders do in fact make little nets. They make little parachutes out of webs if, you know, there's like a flood happening or crazy storm or something they need to escape from and they sail down from the sky. That's the spider update because if you only listen to last episode, it is, you know, it's like a reaper madness of spiders. These scattered spiders, oh no spiders. Let's talk about the opposite. Let's talk about tech news. Again, this is the kind of thing that on the surface, I mean, is really boring. I kind of set off on this podcast deliberately, not attempting necessarily to be like entertaining. I just want to kind of chronicle what it's like to go on a little solo trip.

So I think stuff like just what you're carrying on you. I find interesting because it's so limited. You got your bag, you got the stuff in the bag, every little item is important. And you know, you need to think it through. So to me, this stuff's interesting. But I think, objectively, it's not. I'm just going to ramble to you about a tiny cord.

But that's what I'm here to do. Still going up. Oh, I remember the glory days when I used to, you know, the really deep subways in Montreal and I'd run up the stairs on purpose as exercise.

Now that I have a bad knee, I remember the day. It was in Toronto. There's this really cool in Eastern Toronto. This really cool pathway. It's like a valley, like a gorge that goes down under a bunch of big overpasses. It's quite a nice walk. You can start around warden station or so and head west from there. And I remember there's some steps there and I ran up and down them for no reason just for exercise. And then I went to Montreal right after that. I felt like the next week my knee was so messed up. I had a hard time walking up and down stairs. So now it's like I wear a knee brace and I'm just careful with it. I'm sure it's all tied into my scoliosis and the tilt in my pelvis. It's like an eight degree tilt. And my leg, my left leg, which is the one with the bad knee. If I stand naturally, my right leg, my foot points forward, but my left leg is at a 45 degree angle. I don't know why it just naturally does that. So I've like muscle memory, trained myself to walk with both legs straight. But to me, it feels like I'm duck footed, like I'm forcing my foot, my left foot inward. That's how it feels. But the result is that it's just straight and it does help a lot. It helps my knee not hurt.

But man, all this stuff, it's just like whenever I see athletes that are perfectly symmetrical. I'm just so jealous because it's only the tiniest little thing, the tiniest little tilt in your pelvis that leads to a weirdness in your hip that leads to a dip in your shoulder that leads to a little curve in your back. And as you get older, all this shit just fucks you in the face. So let me know running up these is what I'm saying. Long as I'm careful, it's okay. But same reason why I can't really do push ups and pull ups anymore because it just hurts my shoulder. Because I don't know. I find explosive exercise so much more fun. It's so much more fun to do push ups, do pull ups, do squats, carry a big weight.

It's so much more satisfying but I can't do that anymore because I just get injured. I have to do boring yoga type stuff and I just don't have the mind for it. I just can't commit to it because it's so low impact and so boring. So I guess I get a happy middle with all this walking I do and I just try to be mindful at least while I'm doing this that I'm helping and not hurting my poor body.

So anyway, there's the old man body update. So one of the things, if you've listened to all of these, God bless you. But one of my things is I bought an electric razor like a shaver so I could buzz my hair because I only just ever want to get, I just get my hair buzzed all the way off. Just go into a barber, give me your shortest setting, buzz it off, please. That's all.

I used to cost 10 bucks, I've noticed around here, it's up to 12 or 13 now. But even that, just that everything in Japan when you don't speak the language is shockingly difficult. So even just that was hard, hard to do.

Here's a branch where I could go back down, but the hiking trail arrow points further up, let's go.

So I bought this wireless shaver thing because it was like hair clippers because they weren't sale at Walmart. And they're wireless and I thought it would be worth it to put that in my bag even though space is at a premium. Because the one stupid thing I brought last time I traveled around the earth is I brought an Xbox controller that I could just plug into my computer in case I wanted to play a game on steam. And I never did. I went literally around the earth and I never used it once. So I was like, all right, I'm losing the controller. I'll replace it with hair clippers. That seems worth it. And then I got to Montreal and I went to use this thing and I didn't have the cord, the recharge cord. I just could not believe it. What an idiot. How did I forget that? But I thought I'd hang onto them in case I could find a replacement cord. I'm sure I could very easily get one off eBay. But since I'm moving around all the time, I don't have a permanent enough address to do that. Meanwhile, in Montreal, I got like a really good pair of hair clippers. Big, heavy metal ones for $12 at a thrift store. But the plug-in ones had the dual problem of having to plug them in. Difficult to do. It's a lot easier to just go in the shower and buzz your hair while you're crouched in a tub so you can easily clean up the hair. But if you got a plug stuff in, that's a lot harder to do. Because I went back to Akiruno because you know, I wanted to relive my old adventures. But I think I've done that pretty successfully. But I bet that's pretty cool. So I'm going to go do that. And I'm going to step aside and let these people pass me.

Alright, the ridge is called the Sakura Ridge, which something tells me it's not the only Sakura Ridge in Japan. It's like I'm making it up like, I don't know, Sakura Ridge. Why not? But there's also something called Observatory Square. So I got to assume that's pretty cool. So I think this will be a fine completion of the adventure. This is fine. Just assume this is the adventure because I did think this was going to be the adventure. I didn't know that Congo falls and all this stuff. And this is generally how exploring goes is you don't make it the first time. The first time is a reconnaissance mission just to learn that this stuff even exists. Next time, I can try again. Because yeah, I remember in Fukuoka. I mean, in Fukuoka, I was really kind of forging my own path almost, not really. I mean, they were paths there, but nothing official, nothing signposted, nothing really touristy. It took me a couple of days just to figure out how to get into the mountains. I was like, the right there is like incredibly hard. And then all the different paths through the mountains and routes and stuff.

This one's a lot easier than that.

And yeah, I mean, maybe. I don't know. I just wonder if I forced myself to get up really early just so I can make this. Will I just be goddamn miserable? I just, I don't function well. I have a hard time seeing the joy in life when I'm dead tired.

So I don't know. I'm really feeling, this is the first time I'm feeling a little trapped by the time of year. I loved walking around the city and having it get dark at five o'clock. It's cool. It's like, wow, I get to see the nighttime version of Tokyo without staying up all night. Win, win. But when you're coming out into the woods, that's fucking awful. It's real downside.

There's plenty to those two guys that just passed me on the path. That brought back a little Fukuoka memory of, well, even just at the bottom here with the old folks that were having their little picnic, I definitely don't mind being ignored. It's actually one of the things I kind of like about Japan that I can just walk around like a ghost and no one pays attention to me and they almost actively act like I'm not there. And I don't mind. I'm just, again, I'm just the guy who's, like, I've read many things about people who try to move here, Westerners who try to become part of Japan and have frustrating, they find it. Because no matter how long they're here, even if they live here and they have property here and they can speak the language and they have a job, their citizens even, they just never get treated like Japanese people or people kind of act like they're not there or don't speak to them in Japanese and all this stuff. And I'm sure that is frustrating, but I also think it's an odd thing to try to do. You know, it just seems like an odd desire in your life to be Japanese if you're not Japanese. 1:05:00 If you want to move to America and be American and take part in the great American dream, like that's what America is for. That's what America wants people to do. That's their whole culture where it seems extremely evident that that is not at all what Japan is interested in having anybody do. You know, I guess with like declining birth rates and stuff there, more and more interested in people moving here, but that doesn't mean they're interested in everybody being Japanese or being considered Japanese. And I think that's like an interesting thing about the Western mindset is we kind of think of that like, it's like, okay, it'll be tough, it'll be an uphill battle. It's like I'm swimming upstream, it's going to be difficult, but we still expect to win. You know, we expect in the end like, hey, I've spent 10 years every day of my life trying to be Japanese and I'm not, they won't treat me like I'm Japanese. It's like, yeah, because you don't get to win at this. This is way bigger than whatever your weird little personal desires are. This is some ingrained shit, dude. So that is fine with me. I like to be ignored. It's one of the things I actually kind of find a little comfortable about coming to Japan and just walking around and why, and why I haven't really tried to learn the language. And I'm mildly embarrassed when I can't talk to anyone, but it's like, I'm kind of okay because I think that is my place here. My place is to be a tourist, to be a total tourist. I'm really just here to walk around and soak it in.

But it is a little weird. So in the city when everybody ignores me, that's cool. If anything, it's like, I think I've said it before, but it's a little almost strange when I see another foreigner and I do the same thing. I just kind of don't acknowledge them because everyone's just not acknowledging each other. So it feels a little weird for me to just like, hey, I'm just going to acknowledge you, cowboy, because you look like me. But then once in a while, somebody else who's visiting does try to give me the standard little hello nod that we would all normally do, but I'm not doing it. So it's just a little awkward, not quite sure how to maneuver all that. But then times when there's really nobody around, like here at this park, with all the old people down at the bottom who were having their picnic, I walked past them like I was invisible. And just it was a little weird. It's a little weird not to just do the, at least just a little nod, and they said hello.

But again, I was like, hey, whatever, this is what I signed up for. I can't be all right with it all the other times and then suddenly have a problem with it. It just feels a little weird. But then those guys on this path, that reminded me because this exact thing happened in Fukuoka, but in Fukuoka it was like five or six people. I had forgotten all about this. This is where it becomes like a cartoon to ignore somebody. It's like so affected. It's so weird feeling and just like such a phony thing to do. You can't pretend you didn't see somebody when you're on a fucking mountain path and no one else is around. You just can't. That's like bad comedy. That's a silly thing to do. But these guys did it. It wasn't until I said, hey, hello. That then they look over and they're like, oh, oh, hello. Whoa, can eat you up. It's like you fucking saw me. You saw me fucking, come on, come on. What are we doing here? You know, so when they meet like, you know, I'm down with how you guys act, Japan. I'm down with it. It's fine. Ignore everybody. It probably makes things easier when you have a culture this crowded and this full of people. But just don't act like you don't see people when you're the only people for miles around. It's, yeah, it's just a weird thing to do. Like just come on, come on. You know, there's a happy middle. There's a point when...

Oh, oh, here's a fork in the road with Japanese signs. Okay, I'm going to go a little bit down this fork away from the park, but not too far. Because I think it's the wrong way. I noticed something just now on Google Maps. Very important. Good thing to know is you can in fact tell when a tunnel is happening because it's brown.
